definition: Animal lowers itself (e.g. spider lowers itself using its spider silk
Animal rushes and leaps on another another with bites, pecks or kicks
Animal locates a stimulus of potential interest, and directs its attention (eyes, ears, face) towards it, and often keeping very still to observe the situation
Animal barks or roars at another animal, whereby the head movement is different from different types of calling
Animal is being carried on the back of another animal(s)
Animal is being held in the mouth and transported by another animal
Animal is being held by the mouth of another animal and dragged along
Animal sinks its teeth into the object or animal, but does not eat / feed / chew on the object or food
Animal builds a nest (e.g. including birds and marine animals such as fishes at "seabed"). Include birds fluffing its nest to make it look taller
Other types of calls (e.g. horse neighs) that do not belong to bark / roar or chirp
Animal hides by concealing itself by using materials, colours or illumination
Animal carries objects, another animal or materials on its back
Animal bites and carries an object or animal in its mouth
Animal "runs" after the fleeing animal, however no biting occurs
Animal moves towards a taller structure. Not applicable for the movement of insects (i.e. insect crawling up the branch)
Animal (e.g. snake) makes a coil around itself
Animal displays dominance (e.g. Bearing teeth) between individuals
Animal makes calls and large movement with its body parts, sometimes to attend the attention of its potential partner
Example: Water birds repeatedly "dance" / stand on water
Animal is lifeless and does not move
Animal suddenly changes its posture and launches itself upright (i.e., stands on its hind legs)
Animal parasite exits its host
Animal scrapes the ground with its upper / front limbs. Include burrowing
Any other defensive actions that are not defensive rearing (e.g. primate bearing its teeth to its aggressor)
Animal distracts / disturbs another animal. Sometimes calling for attention. Milder level as compared to fighting or playing
Animal makes a diving action (e.g. bird nosediving in the sky, fish diving back into the water)
Animal kicks another animal with its rear legs (e.g. horses)
Animal tilts its head backwards, typically followed by rocking its head forward
Animal dips only its chin into the water
Animal dips its entire face into the water
Animal stretches its neck
Animal tilts its body to its side (e.g. water bird tilting its body to its side on the water surface)
Example: Lizard lifting itself up from the ground or pushing itself away from an object
Animal flips itself and somersault in the air. Include doing back flip
Animal threads / 'float' on water, without making any major movements
Animal is dying
Include feeding, grazing, and gnawing
Animal makes a successful escape from the clutch, grip,or bite by its predator
Example: Butterfly emerging from cocoon
Animal approaches an object, or explores an area in search for something. Include foraging, investigating, or sniffing
Animal falls from a taller or slanted structure, or trips over an object
Include wrestling
Exclude animal flapping its tail. Example: Bird opening and closing its wings
Example: Elephant flapping its ears
Example: Bird flapping only its tail
Different from escaping, animal 'runs' away from its predator or danger, often with rapid change of direction but without having been caught first
Example: Fish struggling out of water
Example: Animal is being pushed / passed around from one animal to another.
Animal gives birth to its young or release its young into the wild
Animal (e.g. firefly) gives off light
Animal is in the air but does not flap its wings, nor does it nosedive
Animal grooms itself. Include scratching
Animal hangs from a taller structure
Animal exits its shell, sometimes breaking the egg shell bits by bits
Example: Horse curls its lip and flare its nostril
Example: Snake feeling threatened and starts to hiss
Include palm-on-palm
Animal makes small jumping movement from one spot to another
Include piggybacking
Animal is not moving as it is immobilized (e.g. poisoned) by another predator
Animal makes large jumping movement from one spot to another (e.g. from lower to higher grounds), or on the same spot
Animal makes no or minimal movement (i.e., animals staying still and alert)
Animal makes a landing (e.g. bird landing on a branch)
Animal leans on an object (e.g. branch)
Animal licks an object
Animal lying flat on the ground
Animal lying on its side
Animal lie on top of another animal (e.g. snakes). Different from hugging which is for 4-legged animals
Animal holds and 'plays' with the object (e.g. primate picking up a rock and use it as a tool to crack an object)
Animal sheds its outer protective covering
Animal moves from one spot to another. Include insects crawling. Include behaviours that cannot be described in other locomotive terms in the Movement category.
Animal gasps for air
Example: Woodpecker repeatedly pecking on the bark (not food)
Animal grooms (e.g. licks fur) the recipient. The recipient will usually be pre-occupied and may move around while being groomed
Similar to performing allo-grooming, but exclusively to birds which may also pick its feathers
Animal mounts itself onto another animal, typically followed by copulation with another animal
Animal makes movement to attract the attention of its partner
Animal explores the body of another animal
Animal moves and "chases" after its potential partner
Animal playfully plays (e.g. huddle, jump on, jump over, playfully chase or bite) with another animal (e.g. lions rolling around each other)
Animal keeping still and pretending to be dead
Animal pounding its chest
Animal grooms (e.g. licks fur) itself
Predator observes (stalking) and waits for opportunate moment to strike its prey
Example: frogs and toads that puff its throat to attract the attention of another animal
Animal pulling another object (e.g. crab pulling the kelp towards itself)
Snake rattles its tail
Animal lying on the ground or branch motionlessly, sometimes slowly opening and closing its eyes.
Animal makes a defending attack (i.e., "attacks" its attacker back). Include ramming by goats
Different from fleeing, the animal faces its predator while moving away from its predator. Include withdrawing
Animal rolls over on its back and to its front
Example: Bird "running" on the surface of water
Different from attending. Attention to the stimulus / stimuli is not fixated. Animal may continuously move its head to scan its surrounding
Rapid movement of its whole body, typically 'rocking' from side-to-side
Rapid movement of its head only, typically 'rocking' from side-to-side
Animal transfers food / feeds another animal (usually its young or partner)
Exclude other categories in Affection. Include using hands or head to rub the head of its young.
Animal makes its way back into the water with its head upright
Animal sits with its rear limbs on the ground or branch
Animal lying on the ground or branch motionlessly, typically with its head on the ground or tucked into its body, and with eyes closed.
Similar to sleeping but mostly applied to birds sleeping in its nest
Animal makes a spitting action or spits out its food. Exclude spitting venom
Snake spits its venom at an object or animal
Animal spreads itself to make itself look bigger (e.g. octopus spreading its tentacles)
Example: Bird spreading its wings
Animal lowers its lower body but does not sit on the ground
Animal pushes itself up from the ground (e.g. standing up on its fore limbs)
Animal stands still on its hind legs, observes the situation and is ready to attack or escape
Animal is visibly shocked by an event or predator. Can include regurgitating / eviscerating
Example: Bee stinging another animal
Animal struggles from the clutch, grip, or bite by its predator
Animal surfaces onto the water surface
Animal makes a swaying movement, often from side-to-side
Animal swims in the water (e.g. fish), or on the surface of water (e.g. water birds)
Example: Water birds repeatedly swimming in circles, as part of social interaction or sexual display
Example: Primate swinging from one branch to another
Animal 'wags' / moves its tail repeatedly. Similar to elephant flapping its ears
Animal is caught, sometimes struggling, in a trap created by predator (e.g. trapped in spider web)
Animal turns its body around, typically on the spot
Animal undergoes chrysalis but does not exit the cocoon
Example: Horse unmounts from the back of another horse
Animal unrolls itself from its rolled state
Animal moves from one spot to another in a slow pace
Example: Bird "walking" on the surface of water
Animal uses water to wash itself (e.g. splashes water on itself)
Animal makes a hand movement, waving with its upper limbs
Animal wraps or coils itself around its prey (e.g. snake coils itself around its prey)
Animal creates an enclosure around its prey (e.g. spider encasing its prey in spider silk)
Animal makes a yawning action, with its mouth wide open and then closed
